data.title: Ollie's Bargain Outlet Holdings: Long-Term Growth Story Remains Intact
data.description:
Investment action

I had a buy rating for Ollie’s Bargain Outlet Holdings (NASDAQ:OLLI) previously, as I continue to see a clear growth story playing out, with solid execution and healthy underlying demand. After reviewing the latest quarter, I stay buy-rated. OLLI is capitalizing aggressively on competitor exits, accelerating store expansion, and leveraging its loyalty program to drive transaction-led CSS. With a broader customer base, more younger shoppers coming into the ecosystem, and a strong balance sheet, I see the long-term growth story intact.

Earnings update

In 2Q25 net sales were $679.6 million, up 17.5% y/y, with comparable store sales [CSS] up 5% on higher transactions. Unit growth also contributed well with 29 openings in the quarter, ending at 613 stores across 34 states, up 16.8% y/y. Margins progressed well too. Gross profit was $271.3 million, and gross margin reached 39.9%, up 200 bps y/y, primarily from lower supply chain costs and a stronger merchandise margin. SG&A did step up to 25.8% on higher medical and casualty claims and slightly higher store labor, but operating income still came in at $77 million with operating margin expanded to 11.3% (up ~80 bps y/y), and net income was $61.3 million with diluted EPS of $0.99; adjusted EPS was also $0.99, up 26.9% y/y. Management then upgraded guidance, now expecting openings to be 85 (from 75), net sales to be $2.631–$2.644 billion, CSS to be 3%–3.5%, gross margin to be 40.3%, and adj. EPS to be $3.76–$3.84, all assuming current tariffs hold.

Broad-Based Demand and Resonating Value Proposition

Digging into the details, the quality of OLLI’s 5% y/y CSS growth makes the result even more encouraging. The noteworthy aspect here is that this growth was transaction-led, with the average basket size essentially flat. For a value retailer, I take this as the healthiest form of growth, as it reflects a broader customer base and increased visit frequency rather than simply leaning on higher pricing. CSS cadence intra-quarter was also very encouraging, with May flat, followed by momentum built in June, and July was strongest. Importantly, management confirmed that transaction growth has carried into August.

The other important takeaway I had was that OLLI's value proposition looks to be widening beyond the traditional core customer. Management highlighted strength in new shoppers from mid-to-upper income brackets, which is perhaps the clearest evidence of a “trade-down” effect in the current environment. At the same time, OLLI’s targeted digital marketing is working well at bringing younger consumers into its ecosystem—on the 2Q25 earnings call, management confirmed that OLLI’s customer file is getting younger in the existing customer base. This has very important long-term implications since it relates to the longevity of the business (i.e., long-term brand relevance since younger generations are eventually going to be the main bulk of consumers).

In my view, this dynamic where OLLI is capturing more affluent, value-seeking households while at the same time capturing more younger shoppers really strengthens OLLI’s growth outlook.

Accelerating Market Share Capture Through Stores and Loyalty

Drawing back to one of the points I made in my last post—the tailwind from competitor exits—OLLI is clearly capitalizing on this opportunity, and it is doing even stronger than what I had expected. We can see this from the pace of expansion in 2Q25, wherein OLLI opened 29 new stores in 2Q25 and 54 in 1H25 alone, which is already above its last full-year record. It makes a lot of sense for OLLI to accelerate expansion here since the faster OLLI moves to capture the white space, the faster it can secure market share. The momentum is not stopping. In the call, management raised its FY25 new store target to 85, which equates to a 15.2% y/y unit growth rate, implying another 31 stores to be opened in 2H25. Importantly, this is not just a one-off acceleration. Management has already suggested that another year of elevated openings is likely in 2026, which basically makes this a multi-year growth catalyst rather than a short-term land grab.

What makes this push to expand the store base more attractive than it looks is OLLI’s Army loyalty program. In 2Q25, membership grew 10.6% y/y to 16.1 million, with loyal customers accounting for >80% of sales (same as 1Q25), and the real development that got me bullish here is the revamp of the “Ollie’s Days” event into a member-exclusive promotion. This revamp was a major success, as it contributed ~100 bps to quarterly CSS growth while being accretive to earnings. In other words, the event drove both revenue growth and was profitable. To give a better sense of how successful this event was, consider that this June event surpassed even the traditional December Army Night, which historically has been the program’s largest event.

Clearly, the flywheel is now spinning faster. The aggressive new store growth is feeding Ollie’s Army, and a stronger Army is fueling the high-value traffic that drives the entire flywheel of more stores > more traffic > more members > more capital to open stores.

Balance sheet strength

OLLI’s has a “fortress” balance sheet to support this entire aggressive growth plan. The numbers say it all. As of the end of 2Q25, OLLI had $460.3 million in cash and investments, up 30.3% y/y, with no meaningful long-term debt. It also has an undrawn $100 million credit facility that adds another $90.4 million of available liquidity. This basically gives OLLI the firepower to fund its FY25 capex plan, which is budgeted at $83 to $88 million for 85 new store openings, while also continuing its share repurchase program, all without leverage. More importantly, I also think it provides good flexibility to capitalize on unique opportunities, like acquiring leases from bankrupt retailers or building inventory to lock in attractive deals.

Valuation

The upside is certainly lesser than when I wrote about this company back in June, but I still think it deserves a buy rating. I believe the strong growth momentum can continue into FY26 (I upgraded revenue growth in FY25/26 to 16%) before moderating back to 10%. And this should justify OLLI to continue trading at 30x forward PE (~4x higher than its 5-year average). I am not being aggressive to assume margin expansion, but I do see potential for that to happen since there is an inherent operating leverage in OLLI business model. At 30x, I have a price target of $148, which equates to a share price upside of 15%.

Risk

There are some risks to note. SG&A did grow faster than sales in 2Q25, up 60 bps y/y, driven by higher medical and casualty claims. Also, the store expansion plans also come with meaningful near-term costs, as pre-opening expenses surged 95.3% y/y. While these investments are necessary to support growth and market share capture, they weigh on near-term profitability. If sales are weaker than expected, it could really hit near-term earnings, which will not be pretty since OLLI is already trading at a premium to history.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on OLLI. The momentum in CSS, transaction growth, and customer file health, coupled with record store openings and a powerful loyalty ecosystem, makes the long-term growth outlook remain attractive. Valuation is no longer as cheap at 30x forward PE, but I believe the premium is justified given the visibility of double-digit growth through FY27.

data.title: Buckle Up For The Biggest Market Disruption Since The Industrial Revolution
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier this week, I participated in the introductory lecture for the Purchasing Management course at my alma mater in the Netherlands. It's been about six years since I took that course, which was part of my master's degree in International Business Administration with a major in Purchasing & Supply Management.

I'm telling you this for two reasons. Reason one is that, although I never worked a day as an actual purchasing manager or supply chain specialist, I have always used these skills in my "Big Picture" research, as everything we do every day is about supply chains. Ever since the pandemic, even "the masses" have found out just how critical efficient supply chains are.

The other reason is that during the Q&A part of the lecture, someone asked me if I had advice for students who were about to enter the workforce within 12-15 months, which applied to nearly everyone in the room.

While I was flattered because this is usually a question someone should ask famous business people instead of me, I said to prepare for a massive AI-fueled disruption, which requires people to focus on finding unique skills that can ideally be complemented by AI but not erased.

That disruption is what I want to discuss today.

In recent months, I have spent a lot of time on issues like "higher for longer" inflation, the Trump administration's desire to "run it hot," where "it" refers to the economy, and how this comes with both opportunities and elevated risks.

One of these risks is that inflation is getting out of control, pressuring consumers without considerable assets to ease the pain. From a social and economic point of view, this is one of my biggest concerns.

We have also spent considerable time on artificial intelligence, as I am a big fan of investing in so-called "AI bottlenecks" like power, infrastructure, suppliers, and water, and because I am convinced that most people are still massively underestimating the power of AI and what this could mean for the economy, you, me, and everyone else.

So, as we have a lot of important developments to discuss, let's get right to it!

The Death Of The American Dream

What's the American dream?

To some, it's owning an AMG Mercedes and a vacation home in Naples, Florida.

Others consider it to be a situation where one is raising a family in a paid-off home in a safe suburban neighborhood with good schools, two cars, and the ability to go on vacation once or twice every year.

Interestingly enough, and using the words of the George W. Bush Institute, it's whatever you want it to be, as it's essentially the opportunity (equal for all) to achieve your dreams:

No less an authority than the Oxford English Dictionary defines the American dream as “the ideal that every citizen of the United States should have an equal opportunity to achieve success and prosperity through hard work, determination, and initiative.” - GWBI

Now, that dream seems to be under tremendous pressure, as The Wall Street Journal wrote in a recent exclusive piece titled "Americans Lose Faith That Hard Work Leads to Economic Gains, WSJ-NORC Poll Finds."

I think the first two paragraphs speak for themselves:

A new Wall Street Journal-NORC poll finds that the share of people who say they have a good chance of improving their standard of living fell to 25%, a record low in surveys dating to 1987. More than three-quarters said they lack confidence that life for the next generation will be better than their own, the poll found.

Nearly 70% of people said they believe the American dream—that if you work hard, you will get ahead—no longer holds true or never did, the highest level in nearly 15 years of surveys. - WSJ

The chart that goes with it is quite something:

Interestingly enough, one might make the case that consumers should be upbeat, as the predicted consumer sentiment shows. This indicator looked at unemployment, inflation, consumer spending, and the stock market.

However, the actual consumer sentiment number is horrible.

In order to explain the gap, one doesn't need a Ph.D. or an IQ of more than 120, as most of us know the economy consists of two camps, the haves and the have-nots.

So, let's quickly explain the gap between predicted and actual consumer sentiment:

  • Unemployment is indeed low. However, people do not base their expectations on current conditions but on what may be in the future. That's how our brains work. Right now, the fear is that AI is going to wreck many job opportunities. These fears are fully warranted, as most successful AI models are already smarter than humans. As we can see below, demand for jobs with high AI disruption risks has truly imploded. We also see that demand for young people has fallen, as people who come out of college often have only theoretical knowledge, something that AI competes with.

Between you and me, the two charts above worry me greatly.

  • Inflation is at 2.7% (all items). While it's 70 basis points above the Fed's target, it's not the worst number and definitely not something that screams the end of the American dream. However, we need to be aware that inflation has been above the Fed's target since 2021. Moreover, as I have often written, odds are that inflation remains higher for longer due to secular trends like the end of the U.S. shale revolution, deglobalization, tariffs, and massive AI spending.

I combined the next two items in one bullet point.

  • Consumer spending/stock market (wealth) are huge drivers of the American dream. Unfortunately, the stock market's all-time high has mainly benefited the people with substantial assets. The same goes for the housing market, which is a great wealth builder. Because of elevated home prices and high-interest rates, housing affordability has fallen off a cliff.

In other words, people are fearing for their jobs, unable to buy a house, and not really benefiting from the stock market's massive gains.

All of this may soon get worse.

Buckle Up, AI Is About To Disrupt

Have you heard of Leopold Aschenbrenner?

He's a 23-year-old German-born AI "influencer" who graduated from Columbia as valedictorian at 19 with triple degrees in math, stats, and economics.

Now, he runs a multi-billion-dollar fund in San Francisco called "Situational Awareness," which is named after his AI thesis. Essentially, he is investing with a similar strategy to mine, as he invests in global stocks that benefit from the development of AI, including semiconductors, infrastructure, and power companies. He's shorting companies in industries that may be disrupted.

In the first six months of this year, he made 47%.

Investments include the semiconductor VanEck ETF (SMH), Broadcom (AVGO), Intel (INTC), Vistra Corp. (VST), Core Scientific (CORZ), EQT Corp. (EQT), Constellation Energy Corp. (CEG), Applied Digital Corp. (APLD), and more.

The reason I'm bringing all of this up is because Aschenbrenner predicts an "intelligence explosion" where AI automates AI research, compressing decades of progress into years. He frames this as a national security imperative for the U.S. and warns of risks from authoritarian regimes like China, espionage, misalignment, and weaponization.

You can read his full essay here.

Aschenbrenner, who is way smarter than me on this subject, brings up one thing I have said over and over again, which is that AI will become much more powerful than one can imagine, as it could fit decades of algorithmic progress into just one year, which would massively accelerate innovation beyond anything we can currently comprehend.

Needless to say, this includes worker replacement, as AI models are already smarter than most people. For now, we're lucky that AI systems do not function as standalone applications. At least in most business settings.

Especially as we enter the age of superintelligence, which is nothing compared to the GPT-4 capabilities we currently enjoy, the disruption really starts to take off, likely soaring somewhere between 2026 and 2028.

As one can imagine, this could lead to mass unemployment in cognitive sectors, although he notes a potential "long tail" for full automation and initial human oversight. Once we are further in the superintelligence phase, dealing with AI will be like dealing with an actual human being.

By the end of this, I expect us to get something that looks a lot like a drop-in remote worker. An agent that joins your company, is onboarded like a new human hire, messages you and colleagues on Slack and uses your softwares, makes pull requests, and that, given big projects, can do the model-equivalent of a human going away for weeks to independently complete the project. - Situational Awareness

Moreover, he calls for an industrial revolution, as industrial sectors are often constrained by labor. Currently, that's one of the reasons why many large projects are slow to get off the ground. Once these labor bottlenecks are slowly solved by robotics and mass automation, we could see a massive CapEx boom and a widening physical footprint of many companies. That's on top of hyper-fast innovation in areas like healthcare, computing, and anything else that AI can rapidly accelerate.

For companies, disruption means adaptation or extinction. As straightforward as that may sound, it's what we are dealing with. According to the report, trillion-dollar cluster buildouts create winners (e.g., AI leaders with $10T valuations), but automation forces workflow changes and potential government mergers to limit private control.

Painting with a broad brush, here are some of the things we could encounter when it comes to disruption:

  • Pharmaceutical companies could use AI to develop new drugs within weeks, often tailoring drugs to individual needs, something that current drugs are not capable of (and why many fail).
  • We could disrupt the entertainment industry by simply asking AI to generate shows, movies, and games for us. All tailored to our desires.
  • Trucks, cars, and taxis could all become fully remote, limiting self-driving cars to tracks. The same happened to horses.
  • Finance and banking could become fully decentralized.
  • We could all use AI to create websites and complex programs within minutes.

What does this mean for car companies, consumer brands, industrial companies, healthcare, defense, government organizations, and so much more?

I don't have the answer, but I know for sure that disruption risks have not been this great since the start of the Industrial Revolution, especially when it comes to labor market risks. While the Internet did a number on many companies that were unprepared, it was never a major risk for the job market.

On top of that, I also believe every single investor is required to check their portfolios line by line and check for disruption risks. Although we don't know yet how powerful AI could become, we should approach this with some creativity and assess the pros and cons of AI on every investment we have.

It also explains why investors are paying 22.5x forward earnings for the S&P 500. As the market now expects the biggest surge in margins in decades due to the power of AI, there really isn't a good alternative to American stocks, especially if we consider that the nation is the current AI leader.

However, in order to maintain its leading position, the country needs to address critical challenges like power access and grid modernization. It also has to tackle social issues like employment support while implementing new measures to stay ahead in global defense.

After all, we can assume the widespread adoption of hyper-intelligent AI by other nations could increase the risk of weapons of mass destruction, as highlighted in the Social Awareness paper.

So many issues and not enough answers, which is why I stick to my strategy:

  • Focus on ultra-wide moat companies that have competitive advantages that are unlikely to be broken by AI. It's hard to assess, but we have to give it a shot.
  • While I expect market strength to broaden and the equal-weight S&P 500 to outperform the market-weighted (the "normal") S&P 500, I believe the current market valuation needs to be taken with a grain of salt.
  • I am putting more money to work in the AI supply chain soon. This includes infrastructure, power, midstream, and others.
  • I am prepared to focus a lot more on Big Picture issues tied to AI, as I believe social and geopolitical issues will rapidly evolve in the years ahead.

That said, as serious as all of this is, I'm very upbeat about the future and believe if we put some energy into (literally and figuratively), we'll be just fine.

This goes for people who are about to enter the workforce soon and retirees wondering what AI disruption may mean for their dividend income.

For now, however, this is my takeaway:

Takeaway

My takeaway is that we are in the early innings of the most significant economic disruption since the Industrial Revolution, fully fueled by AI.

The American Dream is already under pressure from inflation and a terrifying gap between the haves and have-nots.

On top of that, an intelligence explosion is coming that could compress decades of progress into just a few years. As dramatic as all of this may sound, I think it's truly happening. Also, it's a national security risk with substantial social consequences on top of an economic earthquake.

For investors, this means we are required to check every line of our portfolios for disruption risks while trying to capitalize on AI without buying into small startups that won't likely survive. To me, this means buying power, infrastructure, and non-AI companies that have the ability to implement it.

For students entering the workforce, my only advice is to find a unique skill that can be complemented by AI. I would also suggest investing every penny in the stock market to build wealth, as I think job security is a thing of the past. At least for most white collar workers, including me.

That said, while the path ahead is uncertain, I'm not worried.

As long as we know what's going on, adapt accordingly, and don't forget to enjoy every day we're alive, I think we'll all be just fine.

(Don't worry, be happy)

data.title: Interactive Brokers: The Brokerage Flywheel Turning Faster With Every New Account
data.description:
Introduction

Interactive Brokers (NASDAQ:IBKR) is one of the world’s leading digital brokerage firms that gives its users access to more than 160 markets in 36 countries and 28 currencies. It caters to retail traders as well as professional money managers and hedge funds. With its relentless approach of automating everything that can be automated in its infrastructure, the company achieves margins that some software firms can only dream of while simultaneously offering the stability of a financial stock. With user growth that is crushing the competition, powered by constant innovation and a customer-first mentality, Interactive Brokers is set to take the market by storm, not just in the US but globally. In this article, I will explain to you why "the company has positioned itself in the right place at the right time" to benefit from structural tailwinds driving the brokerage market today.

Thesis

Interactive Brokers is experiencing rapid growth in their customer base fueled by rising interest in financial markets. As the company not only provides one of the most advanced trading platforms in the market today but also offers a more appealing pricing structure than competitors, I expect this growth to continue. As more and more retail traders are growing their account balances to levels that can take advantage of direct execution capabilities offered by IBKR, they will ultimately move away from platforms like Robinhood (HOOD) that are not only less functional but also have significant conflicts of interest because of their Payment-For-Order-Flow (PFOF) mechanisms, which ultimately hurt the customer. As the number of client accounts grows, so will the income Interactive Brokers earns on their cash balances and margin loans. Because net interest income (NII) is less sensitive to falling interest rates than many might think, account growth will far outweigh the effect that lower rates will have on interest earnings. Last but not least, I believe that Interactive Brokers sits at the forefront of innovation with constant improvements to their platform, i.e. offering prediction contracts that are expanding the scope of what investors can trade in their accounts. Combine that with a less than demanding valuation considering the growth potential and structural tailwinds that the company is facing, and I consider Interactive Brokers a highly attractive investment opportunity today.

Without further ado, let’s dive into the reasons that make me believe that IBKR has much more room to run!

Feeding From Trading Activity

As an online brokerage firm, Interactive Brokers offers their users the ability to trade financial instruments on their platform, including stocks, bonds, options, or currencies. They are offering that functionality in two variants: IBKR Lite and IBKR Pro. While the first one is comparable to the likes of Robinhood and caters more to retail traders that are relatively new to the market, the latter is more geared towards advanced investors that have a need for more pronounced functionality. The appeal of the Lite version centers around zero commission trading that attracts casual traders who are prioritizing simplicity and a low-cost structure. On the other hand, the Pro variant, while not offering commission-free trading, could potentially provide the user with lower costs in the end by minimizing the spread charged on trades through a technology the firm calls ‘SmartRouting’. It automatically searches for the best marketplace to execute the order for the lowest possible price. If you are placing orders that are relatively high in absolute value, this can be a pretty substantial advantage, which is why sophisticated investors usually choose this option. On top of that, the sheer amount of different functionalities on the Pro model is miles ahead of the Lite version because it was built with professionals in mind in the first place.

On a headline level, the economics of the business are relatively straightforward to model. Interactive Brokers as of Q2 2025 has 3.87m active accounts. Every single account makes on average 194 trades per year, while the average commission charged on every trade lies at $2.64. If we multiply this, we get to around $500 of trading commission for each account per year, or $2bn in total. Commission income represents roughly 35% of total revenue as of Q2 2025. As the number of trades as well as the charged commissions will be pretty stable over time, the ultimate driver for upside is going to be account growth.

As we can see in the chart above, the number of active accounts has about tripled over a 4 year time horizon, and the momentum does not appear to be slowing down. This is fueled by a growing interest in financial markets in the younger generations. As the chart below shows, the share of individuals below the age of 40 transferring funds to investment accounts has gone up tremendously since the start of the pandemic. This demographic is the one that Interactive Brokers is after and which will be crucial for their continued growth in customer accounts.

If we compare this to Robinhood, the company reported a 10% y/y increase in the number of active investment accounts in their recent Q2 earnings results. This compares to the 32% y/y growth that IBKR reported in their own customer base. With that said, I think it’s helpful to understand the difference between the business models of IBKR and HOOD. Robinhood uses a model called ‘Payment-For-Order-Flow’ or PFOF.

What that means is, that Robinhood doesn’t route your order directly to an exchange but rather to a market maker, i.e. Citadel. This market maker executes the order and charges a spread that impacts you directly because you are buying or selling at slightly more unfavorable prices. In exchange for receiving the order, the market maker pays the broker a small fee, as he is ‘paying for the order flow’. As execution risk is transferred over to the market maker, costs can be held low, which allows for the offering of zero commission trading. The problem is, that this creates a conflict of interest. The broker is not necessarily acting in the best interest of its customers, as it will route the order to the market maker paying him the highest fee instead of the one that is executing at the best possible price. Therefore, while it might seem attractive to get commission-free trading and theoretically could also be advantageous for customers with lower account balances, if orders are high enough, this might become a real issue. Because of the mentioned conflict of interest, PFOF is illegal in Europe and the UK.

My thesis is that retail traders will start off at Robinhood or use IBKR Lite, and then gradually make the move over to the more advanced version, IBKR Pro, as it will be a far better fit for portfolios with higher account balances, as those will likely take full advantage of the better trading conditions and extensive functionalities.

This theory is further underlined if we take a look at the average account balance of IBKR customers and compare it to Robinhood’s users. As of March 2025, the estimated average account balance on IBKR lies at $158,000. For Robinhood, this number is far lower, with an estimated average account size of well below $10,000. While competitors like Charles Schwab or Fidelity have higher account sizes than Interactive Brokers, those are typically used for different purposes, like 401k plans. As a result, trading activity is significantly lower and therefore commission income will be as well.

Interactive Brokers acts as a natural hedge against rising volatility in the market. Similar to exchanges like CME Group (CME) or Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), in times of heightened uncertainty, trading volumes usually rise.

As can be seen in the chart above, volatility represented by the CBOE Volatility Index (VIX) can spike massively in times of market uncertainty. This was last seen in April, when the announcement of broad tariffs crushed the markets. However, exchanges or brokers typically do not care what type of trade you make as long as you are still trading. Because Interactive Brokers offers a variety of instruments that can be deployed to hedge risk, like options or futures, activity was still strong as the company reported an increase of +63% y/y in Daily Average Revenue Trades (DARTs).

Favorable Customer Mix

One thing to note is that Interactive Brokers has a customer mix of around 55% retail traders and 45% institutional clients, including hedge funds, RIAs and ‘prop traders’. These institutional customers are very valuable for IBKR because they are trading more frequently on average, have higher account and cash balances, and are more interested in using margin loans for trading or securities lending, hence creating more monetization potential than retail accounts.

Additionally, Interactive is offering their infrastructure to other brokers, which they are calling ‘Introducing Brokers’, so they can white-label the platform. In essence, the Introducing Broker is managing their customer relations with account openings, support, and marketing, while IBKR sits in the background and does all the heavy lifting like trade execution and clearing. With that, the Introducing Broker can offer their clients a leading platform without the need for multi-year investments to build up its own solution. For Interactive, this model is highly profitable, as they are taking a share of commission revenues and NII without having to spend a single dollar on customer acquisitions.

The Gift That Keeps On Giving

The second leg of Interactive’s earnings power is their Net Interest Income (NII), which represents roughly 60% of total revenues as of Q2 2025. Basically, the company has three different drivers of NII that it can use to their advantage. First, IBKR takes cash from their customers accounts that is currently not invested and puts it into treasuries or overnight REPOs. By doing that, it earns a market rate of return. Most of that return is flowing back to the customer, as Interactive is offering that market rate minus a spread, which currently sits at 0.5% for the Pro version and 1.5% for the Lite option. For cash balances below $10,000 however, the company keeps the entire return it makes on customer deposits.

There is a misconception that Interactive Brokers will take a huge hit to their earnings potential if interest rates will be going down. In my view this is not entirely true as they are primarily earning a constant spread that will be available to them in both scenarios, a higher-for-longer as well as a falling rates environment. The sensitive portion of that income comes from these lower cash balances where IBKR keeps the whole return as profit. If rates would come down meaningfully, this is where the hit would occur. Regarding the sensitivity to a change in interest rates, the company stated the following during their Q2 2025 earnings call:

A full 1% decrease in all benchmark rates would decrease our annual net interest income by $335m.

If we relate this to their full-year 2024 NII of $3.1bn, $335m would represent a 10.8% impact. As long as rates are not going down to zero again, which I do not project to happen for a long time, NII from cash will scale with overall account growth, as the impact of lower rates will be compensated by a larger customer base.

The second way that IBKR is earning money with NII is by offering margin loans. Customers are borrowing money from Interactive to buy securities with leverage. IBKR is charging them a benchmark market rate plus a spread that will be at 1.5% and goes down with the size of the loan, all the way to 0.5% for loans over $50m. For Lite users, the spread will be constant at 0.5%. Interactive takes all of that spread as profit. As you might have heard in one of their commercials, Interactive Brokers offers really competitive margin loan conditions.

Compared with peers, not only is IBKR offering margin loans far larger, but also at only a fraction of the cost. This is possible because of their core strategic focus on automation. The risk with margin loans is that accounts need to be closed because in market drawdowns the fall in value of the collateral is greater than the value of the margin loan. At IBKR, if the account in that case does not post the required collateral in a very short period of time, it is automatically liquidated and closed without any human interaction taking place. This sets them apart from most of their competitors that have way higher cost for their risk management practices and therefore need to charge higher rates on their loans. Also it allows Interactive Brokers to take on more leverage compared to competition because they are mitigating risk more effectively.

Last but not least, Interactive offers their customers to participate in securities lending. The way it works is fairly simple. If a customer agrees to participate in securities lending, their securities are available for IBKR to lend out to short sellers, who are paying interest for that. That interest rate can vary significantly depending on the security. The key is that IBKR keeps 50% of what it receives from the borrower and gives the other half directly to the owner of the securities. Securities during that period of lending are still fully tradable and collateralized, giving the owner an incentive to join the program.

As you can see, Interactive Brokers is making profits in various ways from earning interest. The key observation is that falling interest rates are not going to hurt them as much as one might think. I would even make the case that they can be a good thing, as margin trading usually picks up as financing conditions are easing, as can be seen in the chart below.

Innovations Will Keep Growth Intact

Constant innovation is the core of long-term growth for any business, and Interactive Brokers certainly understood that. Not only are they investing in their technological capabilities to offer the best trading experience for all kinds of customer groups, but they are also expanding their product offerings. The firm is constantly increasing the number of crypto currencies available on their platform, and has recently considered introducing its own Stablecoin and support for Stablecoin funding.

They also released their brand new desktop version called ‘IBKR Desktop 1.0’, offering advanced features like discovery tools that can translate market trends into actionable investing ideas.

But perhaps their biggest move is the addition of forecast contracts in Canada. These prediction-based instruments are similar to offerings from sites like Polymarket, where the user can bet on outcomes of political, economic or environmental events. The trading of these contracts is available 24/7. Forecast contracts are expected to launch in the US and other regions in the near future.

Q2 2025 Earnings Results

Interactive Brokers released their second quarter earnings results on July 17.

The company increased revenues by +20.3% y/y to $1.48bn and EPS by +15.9% y/y to $1.51, both beating analyst estimates.

Their total active customer accounts grew +32% y/y to a level of 3.866m.

In the quarter, total customer DARTs jumped +49% y/y to 3.55m, driven by heightened volatility in the markets. This led to a rise in commission revenues of +27% y/y to $516m, mostly because of strong performances in stocks, options, and futures.

Net interest income rose by +9% y/y to $860m because of higher average customer credit balances and more activity in securities lending. Customer margin loans increased +18% y/y.

Their pre-tax profit margin came in at an impressive 75%, which represents a 300 bps increase over the year-ago quarter.

Overall, this was a great quarter that is confirming my thesis on continued account growth, structural upside in trading activity in times of higher volatility, and strong earnings power through NII.

Valuation

To determine whether Interactive Brokers is a good investment right now, we have to take a look at the relative valuation versus industry peers.

P/E Non-GAAP (FWD) Net Income Margin (TTM)
Interactive Brokers (IBKR) 31.48 85.76%
Robinhood (HOOD) 60.87 42.72%
Charles Schwab (SCHW) 20.79 40.79%
CME Group (CME) 24.58 76.94%
Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) 25.96 38.59%
Coinbase Global (COIN) 40.64 50.70%
Average 34.05 55.92%

Using a P/E relative valuation method, I calculated the average multiple for the peer group of digital brokers as well as exchanges, which is 34.05 at the time of this article.

This shows that while having far higher margins than peers, the company is currently trading at a discount to the group’s average P/E ratio. While a 31.48 multiple is not cheap and the valuation is also significantly higher compared to one of its closest competitors in Charles Schwab, the unique tailwinds that the company faces are enough to justify that premium in my opinion. It also presents a far better opportunity than Robinhood on the back of a more valuable customer base and superior profitability.

Risks to the Thesis

The primary risk to consider when thinking about an investment in IBKR is related to account growth. If the increase in the number of new accounts suddenly stalls, this would ultimately change the growth projection that I am making. While there could certainly be a temporary slowdown, especially if markets are going down for an extended period of time and investor sentiment follows, I believe that the global opportunity is too great for this rise to come to an abrupt end. A lot of markets are still massively under-penetrated which leaves the door wide open for IBKR to step in and take market share. Another risk that is connected to that is that trading volumes might be going down. However, as markets are cyclical in nature, so are trading volumes. Over time, volumes have been fairly stable and a slight decrease in volumes will most likely be more than compensated by account growth. On the NII side, there is a chance that interest rates will go down more than the 100 bps modeled by the company. In that case, NII could be decreasing faster than anticipated and be a drag on earnings, even if account growth holds up. On the competition side, more attractive pricing by peers could also slow down account growth. Since IBKR is primarily catering to experienced traders, the simplicity and ‘gamification’ character of Robinhood could result in Interactive Brokers failing to attract the younger cohort. Last but not least, as it is the case with all digital companies, technological risks are a constant threat to the business. A major system failure or cyber attacks could lead to financial losses as well as reputational damage.

Summary

Interactive Brokers is growing accounts at a faster pace than competitors while attracting a higher-quality customer base that has larger account balances, trades more frequently, and uses margin loans to a greater extent. The company’s revenue streams are well diversified between commissions and net interest income, offering cyclical upside as well as stability. By focusing on automation as a key business element, IBKR achieves margins that peers can only dream of. With their innovative approach of expanding into new offerings like cryptocurrencies or prediction contracts, growth is expected to continue well into the future. At a reasonable valuation versus peers despite superior profitability metrics, I initiate coverage on Interactive Brokers with a Buy rating.

data.title: Bristol Myers Squibb: A 5% Yield And Single-Digit P/E Still Look Good
data.description:
The Health Care sector was beaten and bruised on a relative basis to the S&P 500 from 12 months ago through this past July. August was a different story, however. The Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLV) outperformed the S&P 500, and actually (along with Materials) was the best of the 11 large-cap groups last month.

Fundamental conditions remain worrisome in terms of earnings growth. Still, that’s largely priced into the sector’s valuation, in my view. Among the cheapest stocks in the space is the Bristol Myers Squibb Company (NYSE:BMY).

I had a buy rating on the $97 billion market cap Pharmaceuticals industry stock in the middle of the second quarter, and reiterate a buy rating. Following a decent Q2 report and some technical developments, I remain upbeat about this single-digit P/E name. Shares are up fractionally since my last analysis.

Health Care Steadying Against the S&P 500

Back in July, BMY reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q2 non-GAAP EPS of $1.46 topped the Wall Street consensus target of $1.10, while revenue of $12.3 billion, up 1% from the same period a year earlier, was a material $890 million beat. It was a double beat and raise.

Shares traded down by 5.8% in the session that followed, however, as the Street questions the overall outlook. Ahead of the October Q3 update, the options market prices in a moderate 5.0% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the report.

Looking back on the quarter that was, BMY's numbers were generally on target, with strong performance from its legacy drugs such as Eliquis, Opdivo, and Revlimid. Keeping my upbeat were decent contributions from a handful of its newer drugs (Reblozyl, Breyanzi, Camzyos, and Cobenfy, among them). Another bullish data point from the report and call? Increased guidance.

The management team raised its FY 2026 revenue guide to be in the range of $45.8 billion to $46.8 billion, up from $46.5 billion to $47.5 billion. Full-year EPS is now seen in the $6.96-$7.26 range on an adjusted basis.

What drove the stock price drop after the report? Perhaps it was a lack of earnings quality given sales numbers by drug, along with pipeline concerns. In company news, BMY appointed a new head of R&D, which adds risk but also potential upside.

What’s more, its partnership with BioNTech aims to accelerate the development of promising new therapies. We’ll have to see how that monetizes in the quarters ahead.

On the earnings outlook, analysts expect weak EPS growth over the years ahead. With per-share operating earnings hovering near $6 in 2025 and through the out-year, it makes sense why the valuation is so depressed.

Furthermore, there has been a slew of sellside EPS downgrades in the past 90 days—24 downgrades with no upgrades. The good news is that profitability trends are very strong, and the forward dividend yield is now 5.2%.

Bristol Myers: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, with ongoing challenges and worries about the pipeline, I am reducing my fundamental price target from $72 to $66. I’m keeping the same $6 normalized non-GAAP EPS figure, but am reducing my earnings multiple assumption from 12 to 11.

That still leaves the stock about 30% undervalued. BMY is about flat since my previous analysis, up 3% compared to the S&P 500’s 9% gain since late May.

BMY: A 5.2% Yield, Very Depressed P/E and PEG Ratio

Key risks for BMY include rising costs related to new strategies and acquisitions, which could sting the bottom line in the near term. Upcoming key data releases and clinical trial dates could bring about volatility, while new R&D management adds further risk. Bigger picture, a faster revenue roll-off from its legacy drugs would be a negative.

At the macro level, tariffs remain not totally known, while the Trump administration’s stance on drugs and vaccines could negatively impact the industry writ large.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q3 2025 earnings date of Thursday, October 30 BMO. Before that, the management team is slated to present at the Morgan Stanley 23rd Annual Global Healthcare Conference 2025 next week, which could draw some share price volatility.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With BMY still considerably undervalued in my opinion, shares remain troubled from a technical point of view. Notice in the chart below that shares are still below their long-term 200-day moving average. That tells me that the bears control the primary trend. Additionally, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph ranges in a neutral zone—I'd like to see a better RSI (between 40 and 80), along with a rising stock price, to help confirm a new uptrend.

But take a look at the short-term 50dma. It appears to be inflecting upward, with price holding above it very recently. Support is seen down at $43, while resistance comes into view between $50 and $51. Finally, with a high amount of volume by price up to the low $50s, the onus is on the bulls to bring BMY through notable resistance.

BMY: Stuck Between Support & Resistance, Falling 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on BMY. I see the pharma giant as sporting a washout-level P/E multiple, high dividend and free cash flow yields, and a chart that has traded sideways for the last two years, underperforming the S&P 500.

data.title: Broadcom Continues To Take Nvidia's Lunch
data.description:
Broadcom (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:AVGO) is already the company we mentioned as part of the next AI wave. Broadcom performed well after hours after the announcement during earnings that the company had a new unnamed $10 billion XPU customer. As we'll see throughout this article, the opportunity in AI isn't in making the shovels and selling it to the miners, it's in helping miners make their own shovels.

Broadcom's central positioning there enables it to drive strong shareholder returns.

XPUs and Broadcom

One of Nvidia's largest risks which we've discussed is that it has massively concentrated revenue.

The risk of this concentration is that the company's customers are massive tech companies. They're companies with substantial resources and assets, assets that we expect them to use to work around Nvidia. In our analogy above, Nvidia is the company making the shovels for miners. Broadcom is the company that's helping miners make their own shovels.

The massive scale of datacenters means that companies can afford to spend to optimize each and every part of the process. Broadcom stands to benefit substantially from that. The fact that Broadcom acquired a 4th customer after its existing 3 XPU customers in a $10 billion deal, shows the scale of opportunity available to Broadcom.

For perspective, Broadcom's first 3 customers are expected to reach 1 million XPUs, which means the potential of $10s of billion per customer in revenue.

Broadcom 3Q Results

Broadcom had strong 3Q results showing the company's continued portfolio strength.

The company saw 22% non-GAAP revenue growth to almost $16 billion for the quarter. That puts the company at more than $60 billion in annual revenue, making it an incredibly large and diversified tech company, with impressive growth. The company managed to achieve even more impressive cash flow growth with more than $7 billion in FCF.

That has pushed the company's annualized FCF to more than $28 billion, putting the company at a 2% FCF yield. While, it's a FCF level that requires growth, the company has a more than 20% growth rate, showing its strength.

Broadcom Diversified Positioning

Broadcom has built up one of the few tech conglomerates with an impressive diversified portfolio.

The company is guiding for more than $50 billion in FY24 revenue, with almost $10 billion in R&D spending. The company's continued investments along with acquisitions have built it a diversified portfolio with ~20K patents. Broadcom's diversified portfolio here makes it incredibly difficult for another company to enter its world.

More importantly, while Nvidia's GPUs can be replicated by XPUs, something already done by Google's TPUs etc., no company has managed to replicate Broadcom's advanced packaging or TSMC's fabs as these technical industries have much higher barriers to entry.

We think the complicated technical nature of XPUs means the barrier to replace Nvidia's GPUs with XPUs is dramatically lower than the barrier to replace Broadcom's packaging.

Shareholder Returns

At the end of the day, what matters is shareholder returns, and Broadcom remains a profitable company.

YoY the company improved its FCF by almost 50%, showing its financial strength. The company is at a 2% FCF yield but with 20% revenue growth YoY and continued expense management, the company can quickly grow to a FCF yield that justifies its valuation. More importantly, the company has yet to realize the benefits of the XPU business.

A lot of the scale that the company has talked about is expected to come in the 2026-2027 time period, into the end of the decade. That means that the company's revenue and FCF growth strength is likely to continue.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is the company's success is predicated on continued substantial AI demand. Even if Broadcom succeeds in taking away Nvidia's lunch, major companies still need to be spending $10s of billions on AI for Broadcom to be earning the amount of funding to drive substantial returns at Broadcom's valuation.

Conclusion

Broadcom has an impressive portfolio of technological assets, and the company just reported strong earnings helping its share price increase by double-digits. The company is incredibly well positioned to take GPU business from Nvidia as the major AI companies can afford to invest at scale in having their own custom silicon.

That strength is shown through the company's win of another $10 billion XPU customer. We expect this business for the company to scale dramatically in the next few years, providing substantial revenue and FCF, and enabling Broadcom to continue to grow its shareholder returns. Putting all of this together, Broadcom is an interesting investment opportunity.

data.title: Timber ETFs: WOOD's Growth Potential Vs. CUT's Defensive Position
data.description:
The timber ETF investing landscape is composed of two ETFs: the iShares Global Timber & Forestry ETF (NASDAQ:WOOD) and the Invesco MSCI Global Timber ETF (NYSEARCA:CUT). I give both of them a hold rating.

Of course, both ETFs will allow you to capture upside thanks to strong global trends of sustainability, decarbonization, and urbanization but the structure of the market caps the upside you can expect. In that regard, thanks to its exposure to emerging and smaller capitalizations, I believe WOOD is the best option.

The Timber Sector’s Recovery

I mentioned sustainability as a key trend, and I believe this is the main interest of timber equities. I have net zero commitments in mind for many countries with a goal in 2050 but (and this is a big but) it was true under the Biden administration, it is not really the case anymore with Trump. The trend remains sound in Europe, and is also gaining momentum in Asia, with delayed targets (2060 to 2070).

The sawlog and wood panel consumption is projected to increase at a CAGR of 3.7% until 2050, driven by innovations like engineered timbers such as cross-laminated timber (CLT) or glulam that can be used as construction materials instead of steel and concrete, resulting in a reduced carbon footprint of up to 35%. This is still a niche market as despite the environmental benefits it still costs 21% more for a standard house in the US, meaning that the adoption will not increase until ways of cutting costs are found. This means that for now, holdings like Weyerhaeuser (WY) and Svenska Cellulosa (OTCPK:SVCBF), found in both portfolio main holdings at around 8% to 10% of the total won’t see a significant upside. The UK has committed to support wood-based constructions, and WOOD has a 5% exposure that CUT doesn’t have.

Portfolios

WOOD is tracking the S&P Global Timber & Forestry Index with a portfolio of 26 companies established in 11 countries. The main focus is on process industries (70.49% of the fund), including major US actors like Weyerhaeuser (WY) and PotlatchDeltic (PCH) or their Brazilian counterpart Suzano (SUZ). The fund is also exposed to Asian markets with Japan (13%) and China (8.56%). The fund has a high top 10 concentration of 54.37% that introduces a high level of risks like wildfire or pest outbreaks. The fund has a focus on emerging actors that could capture higher growth with a 57.57% allocation to mid-caps, a 19.04% allocation to small-caps and only 17.04% to large-caps.

CUT is tracking the MSCI ACWI IMI Timber Select Capped Index with a portfolio of 57 companies and a 48.67% top 10 concentration. As I mentioned in the introduction, this fund is less exposed to emerging markets and leans more into the US (49%), Finland (12%) and Sweden (8%) with a small 1.2% China allocation; the fund is also reliant on process industries (77.36%) and is exposed to REITs at 9.17%, something that differentiates the fund with WOOD. The goal of this constituent is to provide a buffer against price volatility of lumber, but I think it further caps an upside that is already not fantastic. Reflective on its more conservative geographical split, CUT also relies more on well established companies with a 31.76% allocation to large-caps and 54.71% to mid-caps. Small-caps only amount to 11.84% of the portfolio against 19.04% for WOOD.

WOOD is more directly exposed to the demand for lumber while CUT relies more on processing activities. Based on both portfolios, I estimate a roughly 55/45 split for WOOD between direct demand for lumber and indirect demand with processed products and 45/55 for CUT.

Both funds are passive, so differences are coming from their index methodology. The reference index of WOOD is using a market-cap-weighted methodology that gives higher weights to companies with large equity floats while ensuring an exposure to emerging markets (Brazil / East-Asia) that are regions with higher timber demand and price sensitivity. This is a sound strategy as I don’t see that much upside for now in developed markets, but given the 20% exposure to APAC, tariffs in place are adding to the volatility of markets already known to be volatile. I do think that this is an opportunity for a price dip, that could in turn justify a buy rating. For now, the situation is just too tense. On the other hand, CUT’s index has a different methodology. It also follows a market-cap weighted approach but with a 5% cap on single holdings to limit concentration. However, the market is either already consolidated, or consolidating (like in Europe) and I don’t fully agree with that 5% cap. It can be appealing as it forces to get exposure beyond timber and forestry firms, and this is why CUT is holding REITs that WOOD is not holding, and this results in a more defensive portfolio. A key difference is the rebalancing frequency: quarterly for CUT and annually for WOOD. The case for quarterly rebalancing is to avoid ETF drift vs. the market, while annual rebalancing results in 4x less trades and lower transaction fees. Given the low returns of both funds, I like WOOD’s approach more because I don’t mind paying fees for good reasons, but paying fees to a strategy with low returns is something I will never do (returns are in the next part).

Financial Metrics

WOOD’s expense ratio is much lower than CUT’s: 0.40% and 0.82%. WOOD also manages much more assets: the fund’s AUM is $251.4 Mn, nearly 5x more than CUT’s $46.6 Mn. Same goes for the liquidity, WOOD is better than CUT but both are red flags: the daily trading volume of WOOD is 0.2% of its AUM and that of CUT is 0.4% of its AUM. This results in a high 30 Day Median Bid/Ask Spread for both: 0.41% for WOOD and 0.53% for CUT. There really is a liquidity problem in this industry.

WOOD’s dividend yield is low, 1.43% despite its high 30-day SEC yield of 3.30%. I think it is fair to assume that the dividend will soon be increased. CUT’s dividend ratio has the opposite dynamic 3.11% for a 30-day SEC yield of 2.32%. Turning to valuations, WOOD is a lot less expensive than CUT with a P/E ratio of 11.76 and a P/B ratio of 0.94, against 18.22 and 1.30 for CUT.

So far, WOOD appears to be a clear winner, let’s look at the performance:

Both funds are close, WOOD appears to be more volatile despite lower valuation metrics. Overall, historical performances are a disaster, with no value creation over the last 3 years, even destruction for CUT. The short term returns are abysmal and long term returns are barely above 5%.

Since July, both funds have increased: +4.8% for WOOD and +3.6% for CUT. This is a high figure, and not the kind of volatility you would expect from timber stocks in normal times. This is an additional reason why I think that now is not the best way to invest, and thus recommend a hold.

Market Dynamics

The long term demand for timber is supported by several trends, such as urbanization and decarbonization.

The demand for timber is also exposed to interest rates because of the construction sector (that I estimate to account for nearly 60% of the demand for wood), so on this side the outlook is positive. For example in the US housing starts in 2025 were low at 1.3% and the projection for 2026 is 8.6%. The demand in Asia might not recover as fast, driven down by the housing market in China. A good way to monitor trends in Asia can be done by looking at the company Nine Dragons Paper (not in our portfolios) as a proxy for price and volume trends.

In short, in 2026, once the US housing market has picked up and Nine Dragons Paper’s results are pointing in the right direction, it would be a signal to buy WOOD and its 20% exposure to Asia.

The push to paper packaging is fueling the need for timber with a global market projected to grow at a 4.8% CAGR until 2030. In early 2024, the estimated figure was 3 Bn trees per year dedicated to paper packaging, I estimate it to be around 30% of the timber industry, and this figure will grow as the CAGR for the paper packaging industry is above the figure I gave you earlier for the increase of global demand for wood. Two types of woods are used for the packaging industries: softwoods (pine, spruce, fir, and hemlock) and hardwoods (eucalyptus, birch, and aspen).

If we look at our ETFs, they both are significantly exposed to these types of woods, but WOOD slightly more than CUT (50% for CUT vs. 55% for WOOD I’d say). Wood holds companies operating in the pulp, paper, and packaging production such as Weyerhaeuser, Svenska Cellulosa, and Suzano while CUT also includes companies like International Paper (IP), Packaging Corp of America (PKG), WestRock, Smurfit Kappa, and UPM-Kymmene (OTCPK:UPMKF) but with smaller weights.

Risks

One thing that also doesn’t make me want to buy now is the significant risk level, outweighing risks.

I mentioned liquidity earlier, this is an issue for both funds with huge bid/ask spreads: 0.41% for WOOD and 0.53% for CUT.

Both portfolios are significantly concentrated in the top 10, even CUT and its 5% cap, and this exposes both funds to the performance of key holdings and introduces specific risks such as wildfires, pest outbreaks or even regulations. Tariffs are also a risk, and are introducing a high level of price volatility given the uncertainty about future evolutions.

Both funds are subject to interest rate sensitivity, for reasons about the housing market I evoked before, but also because of their exposure to mainly non large-cap companies that are facing tense borrowing conditions and that could ease with the cuts, allowing for aggressive growth in a best case scenario where rate cuts are boosting wood demand by stimulating the housing market and the private consumption, small actors would benefit from favorable financing markets. This is obviously partly integrated in prices today, and a delay in the rate cuts would make both ETF prices tank (which could turn into a buy opportunity).

As both ETFs are exposed internationally, 50% for CUT and 70% for WOOD, a significant part of the returns are not denominated in dollars and the returns are dependent on the dollar index. This is not necessarily a bad thing, as the outlook for the dollar is not the best and UBS recommends holding foreign currencies.

Conclusion

If you want to invest in the timber market with ETFs, you don’t have many options, you will either buy WOOD or CUT. Right now, none of them are a hold because the upside potential is not enough considering the risks, but this could change after a market correction or a change in the market structure.

When that happens, I recommend you pick WOOD as it is positioned more aggressively with smaller companies and in emerging markets, while CUT is more conservative, exposed to markets that are already mature and where I don’t see enough upside.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Wall Street ended the holiday-shortened week mixed as hopes of more aggressive Fed easing this year were balanced with concerns about a recession as the weakness in the labor market was illustrated in sharp relief.

The S&P 500 (SP500) closed up +0.3% for the week, with the Dow (DJI) down -0.3% and the Nasdaq (COMP.IND) rallying by +1.1%.

On Friday, the August jobs report showed an anemic 22K rise in nonfarm payrolls last month, while revisions showed the economy actually lost jobs in June. The markets fully priced in a September Fed rate cut of 25 bps, with a 10% chance the FOMC goes big with 50 bps.

A global bond selloff that started the week reversed by the end. In the Treasury market, fears about the speed of labor market deterioration pushed the 10-year yield (US10Y) to a 5-month low, as it fell more than 15 bps on the week to end at 4.07%.

Gold (CUR:XAUUSD) benefited from the drop in rates, powering above $3,500/oz. and gaining +4% for the week.

The Nasdaq got a boost from Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), which saw its largest non-earnings gap higher open since 2006 after an antitrust court decided it did not have to divest its Chrome browser and could still charge for search inclusion.
